title: Dog Limping After Heavy Exercise: A Triage Guide for Pet Owners
meta_desc: Discover why your dog limps after exercise, the urgency level, and how capturing photos or videos can assist your vet in a diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Limping after exercise, or 'cool-down' lameness, is usually caused by minor injury or underlying stiffness and is rarely an immediate emergency. Rest your dog for 48 hours and seek veterinary care if the limp persists or if they cannot bear weight on the limb.

What causes my dog to limp after heavy exercise?

Limping after heavy exercise is often referred to as 'cool-down' lameness. This occurs when an underlying issue—such as a minor muscle strain, ligamentous injury, or early-stage osteoarthritis—becomes aggravated by strenuous physical activity. While the dog is moving, adrenaline may mask the pain, but once the body rests and the joints cool down, inflammation and stiffness become more apparent to the owner.

Is my dog's post-exercise limping an emergency?

  • The urgency level for limping that only appears after exercise is generally Low if your dog is eating, not vocalizing, and can bear some weight.
  • Provide strict rest and monitor the situation closely for improvement.
  • Schedule a standard veterinary appointment if the limp does not improve with 48 hours of rest.
  • Seek immediate care if the condition progresses to non-weight-bearing lameness.

Why should I take a photo or video of my dog's gait to help with triage?

  • Capturing a video at home is essential because adrenaline at the clinic can often hide a limp from the veterinarian.
  • A video provides a clear record of which leg is affected and the severity of the limp in a relaxed environment.
  • Photos of the paws or specific joints can help identify subtle swelling or abnormalities that may not be obvious during a physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following heavy exercise in dogs, lameness warrants careful assessment. Observe the dog's gait, noting any shortened stride on the affected side or head drop during weight bearing. Systematically palpate the limb from distal to proximal, evaluating bones, joints, and soft tissues for swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, or muscle atrophy. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be necessary for subtle or obscure lameness. Radiography and other imaging techniques such as ultrasonography, CT, or MRI can aid in diagnosis.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain relief like Ibuprofen?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications as many are highly toxic to pets. Always consult your veterinarian for pet-safe anti-inflammatory options.

faqs.question: How much rest does my dog need after a strain?
faqs.answer: Generally, 48 to 72 hours of strict rest (leash walks only for bathroom breaks) is recommended. If the limp persists beyond this window, a professional exam is necessary.

faqs.question: When should I be more concerned about the limp?
faqs.answer: If you notice heat in the joint, significant swelling, or if your dog refuses to put any weight on the leg at all, the urgency level increases and they should be seen sooner.
